lines: +# Inventory — Configurable Rig Templates via Guild Config

-Every writ gets the same hardcoded 5-engine rig (draft → implement → review → revise → seal), regardless of writ type. Different kinds of work need different pipelines. This feature lets guilds pre-compose writ-type-to-rig-structure mappings in guild config. The Spider's `spider` config section gains a `rigTemplates` map. Givens support named variable references (`$writ`, `$role`, `$buildCommand`, `$testCommand`). Templates are validated at startup; current hardcoded pipeline is preserved as fallback.
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` — Primary change site. Four change areas:
+  1. `buildStaticEngines()` — kept intact as fallback; new template-resolution function alongside it
+  2. `trySpawn()` — call site dispatches to template-based engine building vs. static fallback based on writ type and config
+  3. `start()` — adds template validation after reading spiderConfig; CDC handler updated for resolution fallback when no seal engine exists
+  4. Closure-scoped `spiderConfig` variable captures the full config including templates at startup

+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` — New types: `RigTemplateEngine`, `RigTemplate`. Updated: `SpiderConfig` gains `rigTemplates?`. GuildConfig module augmentation already here and picks up the `SpiderConfig` change automatically.
+### Possibly created (or inlined into existing files)
-The main implementation file. Changes required in:
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/template-validator.ts` — pure validation function for configured templates; checks designId registry, upstream references, acyclicity. Could be inline in spider.ts.
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/resolve-givens.ts` — variable interpolation logic; resolves `$writ`, `$role`, `$spider.*` references against writ + config. Could be inline in spider.ts.
-- `buildStaticEngines()` — currently builds the hardcoded 5-engine pipeline. This either gets replaced or called as the fallback when no template matches.
-- `trySpawn()` — calls `buildStaticEngines(writ, spiderConfig)`. Must be updated to call template-building logic instead, looking up writ type → template → resolved `EngineInstance[]`.

-- `start()` — reads `spiderConfig = g.guildConfig().spider ?? {}`. Must add post-read template validation. Since Spider `requires: ['fabricator']` and fabricator is started before Spider, `fabricator.getEngineDesign()` is callable here.
-- CDC handler (the `stacks.watch` block inside `start()`) — **currently hardcodes** `rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === 'seal')` for resolution. Needs fallback for templates without a `seal` engine.
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts` — All existing tests must continue passing. New test cases required for template behavior (see Test File section below).
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/*.ts` — all 5 engine implementations unchanged; they consume `givensSpec` as-is.
+- `packages/plugins/spider/src/index.ts` — re-exports; new types added to `types.ts` will need export entries here if they are public API.
+- `packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts` — no API changes; Spider calls `getEngineDesign()` during validation.
+- `packages/framework/core/src/guild-config.ts` — open via module augmentation; no framework-level changes.
-    { id: 'draft',     designId: 'draft',     status: 'pending', upstream: [],           givensSpec: { writ } },
-    { id: 'implement', designId: 'implement', status: 'pending', upstream: ['draft'],     givensSpec: { writ, role } },
-    { id: 'review',    designId: 'review',    status: 'pending', upstream: ['implement'], givensSpec: reviewGivens },
-    { id: 'revise',    designId: 'revise',    status: 'pending', upstream: ['review'],    givensSpec: { writ, role } },
+  role?: string;          // Role to summon for quick engine sessions. Default: 'artificer'.
+  pollIntervalMs?: number; // Polling interval for crawlContinual tool (ms). Default: 5000.
+  buildCommand?: string;  // Build command passed to review engine givens.
+  testCommand?: string;   // Test command passed to review engine givens.
-- `RigTemplateEngine` — a single engine slot declared in a template (designId, id, upstream array, givens map with possible variable refs)
-- `RigTemplate` — a named template: ordered list of `RigTemplateEngine` entries, optional `resolutionEngine` field
-- Updated `SpiderConfig` — adds `rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>` (keyed by writ type; `'default'` is the special fallback key)
-All existing tests pass unchanged (no regressions). New test cases needed for:
-- Template-based spawn: writ type matching, default fallback
-- Startup validation failures (bad designId, bad upstream ref, cycle detection)
-- CDC completion resolution fallback (no `seal` engine — use last completed engine)
-- Backwards compatibility: no `rigTemplates` configured → hardcoded 5-engine pipeline

-The test file is large (23K tokens), uses `node:test` and `node:assert/strict`, builds its own in-memory fixture with `MemoryBackend`, mock `AnimatorApi`, and `buildFixture()`. GuildConfig is provided via `fakeGuildConfig` in `buildFixture()`.
-To test template config, `buildFixture()` accepts `Partial<GuildConfig>` as first argument — so `buildFixture({ spider: { rigTemplates: { ... } } })` pattern is already supported.
-Exports `SpiderConfig` and other types. Will need to export the new types (`RigTemplate`, `RigTemplateEngine`) if they become part of the public API.
+  givensSpec: Record<string, unknown>;  // always holds resolved literal values, never $refs

-*Note: `givensSpec` currently holds resolved literal values set at spawn time — no template expressions. This feature introduces template expressions that are resolved into literals at spawn time, so `EngineInstance.givensSpec` stays unchanged (always holds resolved values in the stored rig).*
-*No change needed: `RigDoc` stores the resolved `EngineInstance[]`, not the template.*
-*Must be extended with `rigTemplates?` (and possibly `defaultTemplate?` for the default key — TBD in design).*
+Only exposes `getEngineDesign(id)`. No `listEngineDesigns()` or `hasEngineDesign()`. Template validation must call `getEngineDesign(designId) !== undefined` per-entry.
-  type: string;        // ← key field: writ type is used to select template
+  type: string;       // ← key field: writ type is the template lookup key
-*WritDoc.type is already stored. The Spider queries writs at spawn time and has the full `WritDoc` — `writ.type` is the lookup key.*
-*Spider uses `fabricator.getEngineDesign(designId)` at run time. For startup validation, same call is used. No Fabricator changes needed.*
+`writ.type` is always present on a spawned writ. The Spider reads the full `WritDoc` in `trySpawn()` and has it available for template lookup.
-*Module augmentation pattern used by `clerk` and `spider` to add typed plugin keys. Spider will continue using this pattern.*

content:
-
MODE: ANALYST

Here is the brief:

# Configurable rig templates via guild config

## Problem

Every writ gets the same hardcoded 5-engine rig (draft → implement → review → revise → seal), regardless of writ type. Different kinds of work need different pipelines — a spec-writing commission doesn't need review/revise, a hotfix might skip drafting, a doc update might use a different role. Today the only way to change the pipeline is to modify Spider source code. This is a stop-gap before the full needs-discovery system: let guilds pre-compose writ-type-to-rig-structure mappings in guild config.

## Desired behavior

The Spider's `spider` config section gains a `rigTemplates` map: keyed by writ type, each entry defines an ordered list of engine instances with their designIds, upstream dependencies, and givens. When spawning a rig, the Spider looks up the template matching the writ's type, falling back to a default template. Givens support variable references that the Spider resolves at spawn time. At minimum, `$writ` (the writ document) and `$role` (the configured role) are well-known references. Beyond those, the template needs a general mechanism for passing through arbitrary Spider/guild config values to engine givens — rather than enumerating specific keys like `buildCommand` or `testCommand` in the variable vocabulary. Undefined variables are omitted from givens. If no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged (full backwards compatibility).

## Validation

At startup, the Spider validates all configured templates: every `designId` must exist in the Fabricator's engine registry, upstream references must point to engine ids within the same template, and the dependency graph must be acyclic. Invalid templates fail guild startup with a clear error message. The rig-completion CDC currently hardcodes a lookup for an engine with `id: 'seal'` to extract the resolution summary — this needs a fallback for templates that don't include a seal engine (use the last completed engine's yields, or allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution).

---

Slug: configurable-rig-templates-via-guild

27	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/tools/*.ts` — all tools unchanged.
28	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/index.ts` — re-exports; new types added to `types.ts` will need export entries here if they are public API.
29	- `packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts` — no API changes; Spider calls `getEngineDesign()` during validation.
30	- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` — `WritDoc.type` already present; no changes needed.
31	- `packages/framework/core/src/guild-config.ts` — open via module augmentation; no framework-level changes.
32	- All other apparatus packages.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Current Type Signatures (verbatim)
37	
38	### `SpiderConfig` — `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` (lines 142–161)
39

41	export interface SpiderConfig {
42	  role?: string;          // Role to summon for quick engine sessions. Default: 'artificer'.
43	  pollIntervalMs?: number; // Polling interval for crawlContinual tool (ms). Default: 5000.
44	  buildCommand?: string;  // Build command passed to review engine givens.
45	  testCommand?: string;   // Test command passed to review engine givens.
46	}
47	```
48	
49	GuildConfig module augmentation (same file, lines 250–254):
50	```typescript
51	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
52	  interface GuildConfig {

54	  }
55	}
56	```
57	
58	### `EngineInstance` — `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` (lines 25–46)

63	  designId: string;
64	  status: EngineStatus;

67	  yields?: unknown;
68	  error?: string;
69	  sessionId?: string;
70	  startedAt?: string;
71	  completedAt?: string;
72	}
73	```
74	
75	### `RigDoc` — `packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts` (lines 58–72)
76	
77	```typescript
78	export interface RigDoc {
79	  [key: string]: unknown;
80	  id: string;
81	  writId: string;
82	  status: RigStatus;
83	  engines: EngineInstance[];
84	  createdAt: string;
85	}
86	```
87	
88	No `templateId`, `writType`, or resolution-hint fields currently on `RigDoc`.
89	
90	### `FabricatorApi` — `packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts` (lines 80–86)
91	
92	```typescript
93	export interface FabricatorApi {
94	  getEngineDesign(id: string): EngineDesign | undefined;
95	}
96	```
97	
98	Only exposes `getEngineDesign(id)`. No `listEngineDesigns()` or `hasEngineDesign()`. Template validation must call `getEngineDesign(designId) !== undefined` per-entry.
99	
100	### `WritDoc` — `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` (lines 27–52)
101	
102	```typescript
103	export interface WritDoc {
104	  [key: string]: unknown;
105	  id: string;
106	  type: string;       // ← key field: writ type is the template lookup key
107	  status: WritStatus;
108	  title: string;
109	  body: string;
110	  codex?: string;
111	  createdAt: string;
112	  updatedAt: string;
113	  acceptedAt?: string;
114	  resolvedAt?: string;
115	  resolution?: string;
116	}
117	```
118	
119	`writ.type` is always present on a spawned writ. The Spider reads the full `WritDoc` in `trySpawn()` and has it available for template lookup.
120	
121	---
122	
123	## Current Function Signatures
124	
125	### `buildStaticEngines` — `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` (lines 95–111)
126	
127	```typescript
128	function buildStaticEngines(writ: WritDoc, config: SpiderConfig): EngineInstance[] {
129	  const role = config.role ?? 'artificer';
130	  const reviewGivens: Record<string, unknown> = {
131	    writ,
132	    role: 'reviewer',
133	    ...(config.buildCommand !== undefined ? { buildCommand: config.buildCommand } : {}),
134	    ...(config.testCommand !== undefined ? { testCommand: config.testCommand } : {}),
135	  };
136	
137	  return [
138	    { id: 'draft',     designId: 'draft',     status: 'pending', upstream: [],           givensSpec: { writ } },
139	    { id: 'implement', designId: 'implement', status: 'pending', upstream: ['draft'],     givensSpec: { writ, role } },
140	    { id: 'review',    designId: 'review',    status: 'pending', upstream: ['implement'], givensSpec: reviewGivens },
141	    { id: 'revise',    designId: 'revise',    status: 'pending', upstream: ['review'],    givensSpec: { writ, role } },
142	    { id: 'seal',      designId: 'seal',      status: 'pending', upstream: ['revise'],    givensSpec: {} },
143	  ];
144	}
145	```
146	
147	This is the only location of the hardcoded 5-engine pipeline. Must be preserved for backwards-compatible fallback.
148	
149	### `trySpawn` (relevant call site) — `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` (line 321)
150	
151	```typescript
152	const engines = buildStaticEngines(writ, spiderConfig);
153	```
154	
155	This line becomes the dispatch point: look up template by `writ.type`, fall back to `'default'` template, fall back to `buildStaticEngines()`.
156	
157	### `start()` — `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts` (lines 420–461)
158	
159	```typescript
160	start(_ctx: StartupContext): void {
161	  const g = guild();
162	  spiderConfig = g.guildConfig().spider ?? {};
163	
164	  const stacks = g.apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
165	  clerk = g.apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk');
166	  fabricator = g.apparatus<FabricatorApi>('fabricator');
167	
168	  rigsBook = stacks.book<RigDoc>('spider', 'rigs');
169	  sessionsBook = stacks.readBook<SessionDoc>('animator', 'sessions');
170	  writsBook = stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs');
171	
172	  // CDC — Phase 1 cascade on rigs book.
173	  stacks.watch<RigDoc>(
174	    'spider',
175	    'rigs',
176	    async (event) => {
177	      if (event.type !== 'update') return;
178	      const rig = event.entry;
179	      const prev = event.prev;
180	      if (rig.status === prev.status) return;
181	      if (rig.status === 'completed') {
182	        // ← HARDCODED: looks for engine id === 'seal'
183	        const sealEngine = rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === 'seal');
184	        const resolution = sealEngine?.yields
185	          ? JSON.stringify(sealEngine.yields)
186	          : 'Rig completed';
187	        await clerk.transition(rig.writId, 'completed', { resolution });
188	      } else if (rig.status === 'failed') {
189	        const failedEngine = rig.engines.find((e) => e.status === 'failed');
190	        const resolution = failedEngine?.error ?? 'Engine failure';
191	        await clerk.transition(rig.writId, 'failed', { resolution });
192	      }
193	    },
194	    { failOnError: true },
195	  );
196	}
197	```
198	
199	Two changes needed: (1) template validation added after `spiderConfig` is read; (2) CDC completion handler updated to fall back gracefully when no `seal` engine exists.
200	
201	---
202	
203	## Key Constraint: Fabricator Engine Registration Timing
204	
205	**Spider's own built-in engines are NOT in the Fabricator when Spider's `start()` runs.**
206	
207	The `arbor.ts` startup sequence:
208	```
209	1. Load all plugins (kits + apparatus)
210	2. Fire plugin:initialized for all KIT plugins
211	   → Fabricator.start() already ran; it scans g.kits() in start()
212	   → Any engines in kit packages are now registered in Fabricator
213	3. Start apparatus in topological order:
214	   ├── stacks.start()
215	   ├── clerk.start()
216	   ├── fabricator.start()
217	   │   └── scans all kits (done)
218	   │   └── ctx.on('plugin:initialized') → registers supportKit engines from future apparatus
219	   ├── [other apparatus...]  → plugin:initialized → Fabricator registers their supportKit engines
220	   └── spider.start()  ← HERE: Fabricator has all kits + all apparatus started before Spider
221	       └── [validation logic runs at this point]
222	4. plugin:initialized fires for Spider
223	   → Fabricator registers Spider's supportKit: draft, implement, review, revise, seal
224	```
225	
226	Spider's own engine IDs (`draft`, `implement`, `review`, `revise`, `seal`) are added to the Fabricator registry **after** `start()` returns. So:
227	
228	- A template containing `"designId": "implement"` cannot be validated via `fabricator.getEngineDesign('implement')` inside `start()` — it will return `undefined` at that point.
229	- Templates referencing engine designs from kit packages (which loaded before Spider) CAN be validated at `start()` time.
230	- Templates referencing designs from apparatus that loaded before Spider CAN be validated at `start()` time.
231	
232	**Implication for the implementation:** The validation logic must account for this. Options:
233	- A) Validate in `start()` but treat Spider's own builtin IDs as implicitly valid (Spider knows its own supportKit engines at compile time).
234	- B) Use `ctx.on('plugin:initialized')` to defer a single validation pass after all apparatus have loaded — but `plugin:initialized` fires per-apparatus, not once at the end.
235	- C) Validate lazily in `trySpawn()` on first use — functionally "fails early" but not technically "at startup."
236	
237	The brief says "fail guild startup with a clear error message." Option A is the most direct approach that satisfies the intent while working within the timing constraint. The Spider can check `fabricator.getEngineDesign(id) !== undefined || BUILTIN_ENGINE_IDS.has(id)` where `BUILTIN_ENGINE_IDS = new Set(['draft', 'implement', 'review', 'revise', 'seal'])`.
238	
239	---
240	
241	## Variable Resolution Design Space
242	
243	The brief specifies:
244	- **`$writ`** — the full `WritDoc` object
245	- **`$role`** — the configured role (`spiderConfig.role ?? 'artificer'`)
246	- **General mechanism** for passing arbitrary Spider/guild config values (not just the two above)
247	
248	Current `buildStaticEngines` implicitly encodes these mappings as hardcoded logic. The template system externalizes them as explicit given references.
249	
250	**Proposed variable syntax** inferred from brief wording ("rather than enumerating specific keys like buildCommand or testCommand"):
251	
252	| Variable | Resolves to |
253	|---|---|
254	| `$writ` | The full `WritDoc` object |
255	| `$role` | `spiderConfig.role ?? 'artificer'` |
256	| `$spider.buildCommand` | `spiderConfig.buildCommand` (undefined → omit key) |
257	| `$spider.testCommand` | `spiderConfig.testCommand` (undefined → omit key) |
258	| `$spider.<key>` | `(spiderConfig as Record<string, unknown>)[key]` |
259	
260	The `$role` shorthand is equivalent to `$spider.role` but `$role` is listed as a "well-known reference" with a defined default (`'artificer'`). `$spider.role` without the default logic would just return `undefined` if not set — different behavior.
261	
262	**Undefined resolution:** if a `$spider.key` resolves to `undefined` (key not present in config), the entry is **omitted from givensSpec** entirely. This matches `buildStaticEngines` behavior for `buildCommand`/`testCommand`.
263	
264	**Non-string literal values in givens:** The `givens` map in template config (JSON) can contain literal non-string values like `"role": "reviewer"` (a hardcoded string, not a `$ref`). The resolver should only attempt variable substitution for string values that start with `$`.
265	
266	**Example resolved output:**
267	```typescript
268	// Template givens: { writ: "$writ", role: "$role", buildCommand: "$spider.buildCommand" }
269	// config: { role: "artificer", buildCommand: "pnpm build" }
270	// → givensSpec: { writ: WritDoc, role: "artificer", buildCommand: "pnpm build" }
271	
272	// Template givens: { writ: "$writ", role: "reviewer", buildCommand: "$spider.buildCommand" }
273	// config: { role: "artificer" }  // no buildCommand
274	// → givensSpec: { writ: WritDoc, role: "reviewer" }  // buildCommand omitted
275	```
276	
277	---
278	
279	## CDC Resolution Fallback — Two Options
280	
281	### Current behavior (hardcoded)
282	
283	```typescript
284	const sealEngine = rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === 'seal');
285	const resolution = sealEngine?.yields
286	  ? JSON.stringify(sealEngine.yields)
287	  : 'Rig completed';
288	```
289	
290	### Option A: Last completed engine's yields (automatic fallback)
291	
292	```typescript
293	const sealEngine = rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === 'seal');
294	const lastCompleted = [...rig.engines]
295	  .reverse()
296	  .find((e) => e.status === 'completed' && e.yields !== undefined);
297	const resolutionEngine = sealEngine ?? lastCompleted;
298	const resolution = resolutionEngine?.yields
299	  ? JSON.stringify(resolutionEngine.yields)
300	  : 'Rig completed';
301	```
302	
303	Pros: no new template fields, no new RigDoc fields, backwards compatible (seal engine is still preferred when present).
304	Cons: "last completed" engine order may not be meaningful for non-linear pipelines.
305	
306	### Option B: Template declares `resolutionEngine` id
307	
308	Template config:
309	```json
310	{ "engines": [...], "resolutionEngine": "summarize" }
311	```
312	
313	At spawn time, Spider stores `resolutionEngineId` on RigDoc (new field). CDC handler looks it up:
314	```typescript
315	const resolverId = (rig as RigDocWithResolver).resolutionEngineId ?? 'seal';
316	const resolverEngine = rig.engines.find((e) => e.id === resolverId) 
317	  ?? [...rig.engines].reverse().find((e) => e.status === 'completed');
318	```
319	
320	Pros: explicit author control, meaningful for complex pipelines.
321	Cons: requires new `RigDoc` field (a `[key: string]: unknown` index signature is already present, so no type-system blocker), and new template field.
322	
323	### Brief's stated preference
324	
325	"use the last completed engine's yields, or allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution" — lists both as valid. The analyst must pick.
326	
327	---
328	
329	## Template Config Shape (Expected in guild.json)
330	
331	```json
332	{
333	  "spider": {
334	    "role": "artificer",
335	    "buildCommand": "pnpm build",
336	    "testCommand": "pnpm test",
337	    "rigTemplates": {
338	      "default": {
339	        "engines": [
340	          { "id": "draft",     "designId": "draft",     "upstream": [],            "givens": { "writ": "$writ" } },
341	          { "id": "implement", "designId": "implement", "upstream": ["draft"],     "givens": { "writ": "$writ", "role": "$role" } },
342	          { "id": "review",    "designId": "review",    "upstream": ["implement"], "givens": { "writ": "$writ", "role": "reviewer", "buildCommand": "$spider.buildCommand", "testCommand": "$spider.testCommand" } },
343	          { "id": "revise",    "designId": "revise",    "upstream": ["review"],    "givens": { "writ": "$writ", "role": "$role" } },
344	          { "id": "seal",      "designId": "seal",      "upstream": ["revise"],    "givens": {} }
345	        ]
346	      },
347	      "hotfix": {
348	        "engines": [
349	          { "id": "implement", "designId": "implement", "upstream": [],            "givens": { "writ": "$writ", "role": "$role" } },
350	          { "id": "seal",      "designId": "seal",      "upstream": ["implement"], "givens": {} }
351	        ]
352	      }
353	    }
354	  }
355	}
356	```
357	
358	The `"default"` key is the fallback used when no template matches the writ's type.
359	
360	---
361	
362	## New Types Needed
363	
364	```typescript
365	// packages/plugins/spider/src/types.ts — new
366	
367	/**
368	 * A single engine slot declared in a rig template.
369	 */
370	export interface RigTemplateEngine {
371	  /** Engine id unique within this template (e.g. 'draft', 'my-custom-engine'). */
372	  id: string;
373	  /** Engine design id to look up in the Fabricator (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
374	  designId: string;
375	  /** Engine ids within this template whose completion is required before this engine runs. */
376	  upstream: string[];
377	  /**
378	   * Givens to pass at spawn time.
379	   * String values starting with '$' are treated as variable references resolved at spawn time.
380	   * Non-string values are passed through literally.
381	   * Resolved undefined values are omitted from givensSpec.
382	   */
383	  givens?: Record<string, unknown>;
384	}
385	
386	/**
387	 * A complete rig template — an ordered list of engine slots.
388	 */
389	export interface RigTemplate {
390	  /** Ordered list of engine slot declarations. */
391	  engines: RigTemplateEngine[];
392	  /**
393	   * Engine id whose yields to use as the writ resolution summary.
394	   * Falls back to last-completed-engine yields if omitted or the named engine has no yields.
395	   */
396	  resolutionEngine?: string;
397	}
398	
399	// Extended SpiderConfig:
400	export interface SpiderConfig {
401	  role?: string;
402	  pollIntervalMs?: number;
403	  buildCommand?: string;
404	  testCommand?: string;
405	  /**
406	   * Writ type → rig template mappings.
407	   * 'default' key is the fallback for unmatched writ types.
408	   * If absent entirely, the hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged.
409	   */
410	  rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>;
411	}
412	```
413	
414	---
415	
416	## Validation Rules (from brief)
417	
418	1. Every `engine.designId` in every template must exist in the Fabricator's engine registry at validation time (or be one of Spider's own built-in engine designs — see timing constraint).
419	2. Every `engine.upstream` entry must reference an `engine.id` that exists within the **same** template.
420	3. The `upstream` dependency graph of each template must be acyclic (DFS cycle detection, same algorithm as `guild-lifecycle.ts`).
421	4. If any validation fails, throw with a message identifying: which template (by writ type key), which engine (`id`/`designId`), and which rule was violated.
422	5. Validation must run during guild startup (in `start()` or deferred via startup context), not lazily.
423	
424	---
425	
426	## Test File Analysis
427	
428	File: `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts`
429	
430	**Test framework:** `node:test` with `describe`/`it`/`beforeEach`/`afterEach`. Uses `assert` from `node:assert/strict`.
431	
432	**Core fixture builder:**
433	```typescript
434	function buildFixture(
435	  guildConfig: Partial<GuildConfig> = {},
436	  initialSessionOutcome: { status: 'completed' | 'failed'; error?: string; output?: string } = { status: 'completed' },
437	): { stacks, clerk, fabricator, spider, summonCalls, fire, setSessionOutcome }
438	```
439	
440	Spider config injected via: `buildFixture({ spider: { buildCommand: 'echo ...', testCommand: 'echo ...' } })`.
441	
442	**New template tests will use:** `buildFixture({ spider: { rigTemplates: { 'mandate': { engines: [...] } } } })`
443	
444	**Coverage pattern:** Most tests use the default `buildFixture()` (no explicit spider config) and rely on the hardcoded 5-engine pipeline. All must continue to work unchanged.
445	
446	**Existing relevant test groups:**
447	- `describe('spawn') ` — spawn priority, FIFO ordering, writ → active transition
448	- `describe('run')` — engine execution, clockwork vs quick, error handling
449	- `describe('collect')` — session result collection, yield assembly
450	- `describe('CDC')` — rig completion → writ transition
451	- `describe('review engine')` — mechanical checks, session launch, collect
452	- `describe('revise engine')` — prompt assembly, session launch
453	- `describe('rig-show / rig-list / rig-for-writ')` — API methods
454	
455	**New test groups needed:**
456	- `describe('rig templates — spawn')` — template matching, default fallback, no-template fallback
457	- `describe('rig templates — variable resolution')` — `$writ`, `$role`, `$spider.*`, undefined omission, literal passthrough
458	- `describe('rig templates — validation')` — bad designId, bad upstream ref, cycle, valid passes silently
459	- `describe('rig templates — CDC resolution')` — no seal engine → last-completed fallback
460	
461	---
462	
463	## Adjacent Patterns: How Other Apparatus Handle Config Validation
464	
465	### Clerk — runtime validation (not startup)
466	
467	Clerk validates writ type at `post()` call time by reading live config. No startup validation. Error thrown at `clerk.post()` if type unknown.
468	
469	### Arbor — startup validation of requires/recommends
470	
471	`validateRequires()` in `guild-lifecycle.ts` runs before any apparatus starts. Throws with `[arbor] "X" requires "Y", which is not installed.` Spider cannot use this hook (it runs after Spider starts).
472	
473	### Fabricator — no validation, pure registration
474	
475	Fabricator registers whatever it finds; it never validates or rejects designs. Spider must perform its own "does this designId exist?" check.
476	
477	---
478	
479	## Doc/Code Discrepancies
480	
481	1. **`docs/guides/building-engines.md`** documents Clockworks engines (standing-orders, `nexus-engine.json`, `engine()` factory). This is a completely different concept from Spider/Fabricator `EngineDesign` objects. Both use the word "engine." The guide says nothing about writing `EngineDesign` implementations for Spider rigs. This is a documentation gap, not a bug.
482	
483	2. **`docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` line ~92** describes rig completion as when "the terminal engine (`seal`) completes." Actual code (spider.ts line 200) marks the rig completed when `all engines are completed` (not just `seal`). For the static pipeline these are equivalent (seal is always last), but the doc is imprecise. This brief makes the imprecision matter because templates can have non-seal terminal engines.
484	
485	3. **spider.md config section (lines 648–659)** shows 4 `spider` config keys (`role`, `pollIntervalMs`, `buildCommand`, `testCommand`). `rigTemplates` will be a fifth. The doc is simply incomplete for this feature — expected.
486	
487	4. **spider.md "Future Evolution" note (line 627)**: "The givensSpec currently holds literal values set at rig spawn time. It will grow to support template expressions (e.g. `${draft.worktreePath}`) that resolve specific values from **upstream yields**." This feature is narrower: the template expressions (`$writ`, `$role`, `$spider.*`) resolve from **writ data and config**, not from upstream yields at run time. The upstream-yields expression system described in the doc is a different, more complex future feature. These should not be conflated.
488	
489	---
490	
491	## Scratch Notes / Existing Context
492	
493	- No existing spec files in `specs/` directory (this is the first commission to use it).
494	- No commission log or prior art for this specific feature.
495	- `docs/architecture/apparatus/spider.md` line 7 explicitly labels the current Spider as MVP with "no dynamic extension" — this brief is the first step toward dynamic extension.
496	- The brief explicitly positions this as "a stop-gap before the full needs-discovery system." The implementation should be minimal and clean, not anticipating future expansion beyond what's described.
497

input.content:
brief: |
  Every writ gets the same hardcoded 5-engine rig (draft → implement → review → revise → seal), regardless of writ type. Different kinds of work need different pipelines — a spec-writing commission doesn't need review/revise, a hotfix might skip drafting, a doc update might use a different role. Today the only way to change the pipeline is to modify Spider source code. This is a stop-gap before the full needs-discovery system: let guilds pre-compose writ-type-to-rig-structure mappings in guild config.
slug: configurable-rig-templates-via-guild

scope:
  - id: S1
    description: "Template config shape and writ-type dispatch: SpiderConfig gains a rigTemplates map keyed by writ type; trySpawn looks up the matching template, falls back to a default template, then falls back to the hardcoded pipeline."
    rationale: "Core capability described in the brief — the template map and the lookup/fallback chain."
    included: true

  - id: S2
    description: "Variable resolution in template givens: $writ, $role as well-known references, plus a general $spider.<key> mechanism for arbitrary spider config values. Undefined variables omitted from givens."
    rationale: "Brief explicitly requires variable references with a general mechanism, not an enumerated vocabulary."
    included: true

  - id: S3
    description: "Startup validation of configured templates: designId existence check, upstream reference integrity, acyclicity check. Invalid templates fail guild startup with a clear error."
    rationale: "Brief's Validation section explicitly requires this as a startup-time check."
    included: true

  - id: S4
    description: "CDC resolution fallback: when a completed rig has no seal engine, use an alternative strategy to extract the resolution summary for the writ transition."
    rationale: "Brief explicitly calls out the hardcoded seal lookup as needing a fallback for templates without seal."
    included: true

  - id: S5
    description: "New public types (RigTemplateEngine, RigTemplate) exported from spider-apparatus for consumers that inspect or construct template configurations programmatically."
    rationale: "Implied by SpiderConfig becoming part of the public API — template types are part of its structure."
    included: true

1	brief: |
2	  Every writ gets the same hardcoded 5-engine rig (draft → implement → review → revise → seal), regardless of writ type. Different kinds of work need different pipelines — a spec-writing commission doesn't need review/revise, a hotfix might skip drafting, a doc update might use a different role. Today the only way to change the pipeline is to modify Spider source code. This is a stop-gap before the full needs-discovery system: let guilds pre-compose writ-type-to-rig-structure mappings in guild config.
3	
4	  The Spider's `spider` config section gains a `rigTemplates` map: keyed by writ type, each entry defines an ordered list of engine instances with their designIds, upstream dependencies, and givens. When spawning a rig, the Spider looks up the template matching the writ's type, falling back to a default template. Givens support a small set of named variable references (`$writ`, `$role`, `$buildCommand`, `$testCommand`) that the Spider resolves from the writ and SpiderConfig at spawn time — undefined variables are omitted from givens. If no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged (full backwards compatibility).
5	
6	  At startup, the Spider validates all configured templates: every `designId` must exist in the Fabricator's engine registry, upstream references must point to engine ids within the same template, and the dependency graph must be acyclic. Invalid templates fail guild startup with a clear error message. The rig-completion CDC currently hardcodes a lookup for an engine with `id: 'seal'` to extract the resolution summary — this needs a fallback for templates that don't include a seal engine (use the last completed engine's yields, or allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution).
7	
8	slug: configurable-rig-templates-via-guild
9

13	    rationale: "Core of the brief: 'spider config section gains a rigTemplates map, keyed by writ type'"
14	    included: true
15	
16	  - id: S2
17	    description: "Variable resolution in template givens — $writ, $role, $buildCommand, $testCommand are resolved from the writ and SpiderConfig at spawn time; undefined variables are omitted from the resulting givensSpec"
18	    rationale: "Brief explicitly lists the supported variable references and their resolution semantics"
19	    included: true
20	
21	  - id: S3
22	    description: "Startup validation of templates — designId existence in Fabricator, upstream reference integrity, acyclicity, and clear error on failure"
23	    rationale: "Brief: 'the Spider validates all configured templates' and 'invalid templates fail guild startup'"
24	    included: true
25

27	    description: "CDC resolution fallback — the rig-completion handler no longer hardcodes id=seal for resolution; templates can declare a resolution engine, falling back to the last completed engine's yields"
28	    rationale: "Brief: 'this needs a fallback for templates that don't include a seal engine'"
29	    included: true
30

32	    description: "Backwards compatibility — when no rigTemplates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged"
33	    rationale: "Brief: 'if no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged (full backwards compatibility)'"

file.content:
brief: |
  Every writ gets the same hardcoded 5-engine rig (draft → implement → review → revise → seal), regardless of writ type. Different kinds of work need different pipelines — a spec-writing commission doesn't need review/revise, a hotfix might skip drafting, a doc update might use a different role. Today the only way to change the pipeline is to modify Spider source code. This is a stop-gap before the full needs-discovery system: let guilds pre-compose writ-type-to-rig-structure mappings in guild config.

  The Spider's `spider` config section gains a `rigTemplates` map: keyed by writ type, each entry defines an ordered list of engine instances with their designIds, upstream dependencies, and givens. When spawning a rig, the Spider looks up the template matching the writ's type, falling back to a default template. Givens support a small set of named variable references (`$writ`, `$role`, `$buildCommand`, `$testCommand`) that the Spider resolves from the writ and SpiderConfig at spawn time — undefined variables are omitted from givens. If no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged (full backwards compatibility).

  At startup, the Spider validates all configured templates: every `designId` must exist in the Fabricator's engine registry, upstream references must point to engine ids within the same template, and the dependency graph must be acyclic. Invalid templates fail guild startup with a clear error message. The rig-completion CDC currently hardcodes a lookup for an engine with `id: 'seal'` to extract the resolution summary — this needs a fallback for templates that don't include a seal engine (use the last completed engine's yields, or allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution).

slug: configurable-rig-templates-via-guild

scope:
  - id: S1
    description: "Template config shape and writ-type-to-template dispatch — the rigTemplates map on SpiderConfig, the RigTemplate and RigTemplateEngine types, and the spawn-time lookup logic that selects a template based on the writ's type"
    rationale: "Core of the brief: 'spider config section gains a rigTemplates map, keyed by writ type'"
    included: true

  - id: S2
    description: "Variable resolution in template givens — $writ, $role, $buildCommand, $testCommand are resolved from the writ and SpiderConfig at spawn time; undefined variables are omitted from the resulting givensSpec"
    rationale: "Brief explicitly lists the supported variable references and their resolution semantics"
    included: true

  - id: S3
    description: "Startup validation of templates — designId existence in Fabricator, upstream reference integrity, acyclicity, and clear error on failure"
    rationale: "Brief: 'the Spider validates all configured templates' and 'invalid templates fail guild startup'"
    included: true

  - id: S4
    description: "CDC resolution fallback — the rig-completion handler no longer hardcodes id=seal for resolution; templates can declare a resolution engine, falling back to the last completed engine's yields"
    rationale: "Brief: 'this needs a fallback for templates that don't include a seal engine'"
    included: true

  - id: S5
    description: "Backwards compatibility — when no rigTemplates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged"
    rationale: "Brief: 'if no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged (full backwards compatibility)'"
    included: true

content.content:
1	decisions:
2	  # ── S1: Template config shape and dispatch ──────────────────────────
3	
4	  - id: D1
5	    scope: [S1]
6	    question: "What is the config key name for the template map on SpiderConfig?"
7	    options:
8	      a: "rigTemplates — matches the rig concept used throughout the Spider"
9	      b: "templates — shorter, but ambiguous (template of what?)"
10	      c: "pipelines — describes the structure but introduces a new term"
11	    selected: a
12	    analysis:
13	      audience: [patron, operator]
14	      confidence: high
15	      context: "The brief uses 'rigTemplates' explicitly. The Spider's domain language is rigs and engines — rigTemplates is immediately clear."
16	      observable: true
17	      recommendation: a
18	      rationale: "Brief uses 'rigTemplates' by name; consistent with existing Spider terminology"
19	      stakes: low
20	
21	  - id: D2
22	    scope: [S1]
23	    question: "What are the map keys in rigTemplates, and how does template selection work?"
24	    options:
25	      a: "Keys are writ type strings (e.g. 'mandate', 'task'); 'default' is a reserved key used as fallback when no type-specific template exists"
26	      b: "Keys are arbitrary template names; a separate 'typeMapping' field maps writ types to template names"
27	    selected: a
28	    analysis:
29	      audience: [patron, operator]
30	      confidence: high
31	      context: "The brief says 'keyed by writ type'. Option A is a direct implementation. Option B adds indirection that would allow sharing templates across types, but the brief doesn't call for it and it complicates the config."
32	      observable: true
33	      recommendation: a
34	      rationale: "Brief is explicit: 'keyed by writ type'; direct mapping is simplest"
35	      stakes: high
36	
37	  - id: D3
38	    scope: [S1, S5]
39	    question: "What is the full fallback chain when spawning a rig for a writ?"
40	    options:
41	      a: "writ.type template → 'default' template → hardcoded 5-engine pipeline"
42	      b: "writ.type template → 'default' template (no hardcoded fallback — if rigTemplates exists but has no match and no default, fail)"
43	      c: "writ.type template → hardcoded 5-engine pipeline (no 'default' key concept)"
44	    selected: a
45	    analysis:
46	      audience: [patron, operator]
47	      confidence: high
48	      context: "Brief says 'falling back to a default template' and 'if no templates are configured, the current hardcoded 5-engine pipeline is used unchanged'. Option A satisfies both: the hardcoded pipeline is the final safety net when rigTemplates is absent entirely or when no matching key is found. The 'default' key lets operators define a custom fallback without enumerating every writ type."
49	      observable: true
50	      recommendation: a
51	      rationale: "Three-tier fallback covers all brief requirements: type-specific, configured default, and hardcoded safety net"
52	      stakes: high
53	
54	  - id: D4
55	    scope: [S1]
56	    question: "What is the type shape of a single engine entry in a rig template?"
57	    options:
58	      a: "{ id: string; designId: string; upstream: string[]; givens?: Record<string, unknown> } — four fields, givens optional"
59	      b: "{ id: string; designId: string; upstream?: string[]; givens?: Record<string, unknown> } — upstream also optional (defaults to [])"
60	    selected: b
61	    analysis:
62	      audience: [author, operator]
63	      confidence: high
64	      context: "Upstream defaults to [] for the first engine in a pipeline (no dependencies). Making it optional reduces config noise for simple single-engine or first-in-chain entries. The EngineInstance type has upstream as required string[], so we default at resolution time."
65	      observable: true
66	      recommendation: b
67	      rationale: "Optional upstream with [] default reduces config boilerplate for the common first-engine case"
68	      stakes: low
69	
70	  - id: D5
71	    scope: [S1]
72	    question: "What is the field name for givens in the template engine config?"
73	    options:
74	      a: "givens — distinct from EngineInstance.givensSpec to signal it contains unresolved variable refs"
75	      b: "givensSpec — same name as EngineInstance.givensSpec, implying same semantics"
76	    selected: a
77	    analysis:
78	      audience: [author, operator]
79	      confidence: high
80	      context: "In the template, 'givens' contains $variable references that haven't been resolved yet. In the stored EngineInstance, 'givensSpec' contains fully resolved literal values. Using different names prevents confusion: 'givens' is the config-time declaration, 'givensSpec' is the runtime-resolved version. The resolution step maps template.givens → EngineInstance.givensSpec."
81	      observable: true
82	      recommendation: a
83	      rationale: "Distinct names signal the resolution boundary — givens (unresolved config) vs givensSpec (resolved runtime)"
84	      stakes: low
85	
86	  - id: D6
87	    scope: [S1]
88	    question: "What is the TypeScript type for RigTemplate?"
89	    options:
90	      a: "{ engines: RigTemplateEngine[]; resolutionEngine?: string } — engines array plus optional resolution engine declaration"
91	      b: "RigTemplateEngine[] — just the array, resolution engine handled separately"
92	    selected: a
93	    analysis:
94	      audience: [author, operator]
95	      confidence: high
96	      context: "The brief says 'allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution'. This is a per-template setting, so it belongs on RigTemplate. An object type also leaves room for future fields without a breaking change."
97	      observable: true
98	      recommendation: a
99	      rationale: "Object type accommodates resolutionEngine field and is extensible"
100	      stakes: low
101	
102	  - id: D7
103	    scope: [S1]
104	    question: "Should the new template types (RigTemplate, RigTemplateEngine) be exported from the package's public API (index.ts)?"
105	    options:
106	      a: "Yes — they are part of SpiderConfig which is already public, and consumers may need to construct configs programmatically (e.g. in tests)"
107	      b: "No — keep them internal; consumers only interact with RigDoc/EngineInstance which are already public"
108	    selected: a
109	    analysis:
110	      audience: [author]
111	      confidence: high
112	      context: "SpiderConfig is already exported from index.ts. RigTemplate and RigTemplateEngine are properties of SpiderConfig, so they need to be exported for TypeScript consumers to construct valid SpiderConfig objects."
113	      observable: true
114	      recommendation: a
115	      rationale: "Types referenced by the already-public SpiderConfig must also be public for type safety"
116	      stakes: low
117	
118	  - id: D8
119	    scope: [S1]
120	    question: "Should RigDoc store which template was used to spawn the rig?"
121	    options:
122	      a: "No — RigDoc stores the resolved engines only; the template key is not persisted"
123	      b: "Yes — add an optional templateKey field to RigDoc for observability"
124	    selected: a
125	    analysis:
126	      audience: [operator, implementer]
127	      confidence: medium
128	      context: "RigDoc currently has no concept of its origin. Adding a templateKey field would help operators debug which template was used, but it adds a field to a stored document (migration concern) and the same information can be inferred from the writ type + current config. Keeping RigDoc unchanged means zero migration and zero downstream impact."
129	      observable: false
130	      recommendation: a
131	      rationale: "Avoids stored-document changes and migration; origin is inferable from writ type"
132	      stakes: low
133	
134	  # ── S2: Variable resolution ─────────────────────────────────────────
135	
136	  - id: D9
137	    scope: [S2]
138	    question: "How are variable references represented in the template givens config?"
139	    options:
140	      a: "String values starting with '$' are treated as variable references (e.g. \"$writ\", \"$role\"); all other values (strings not starting with $, numbers, objects, booleans) are passed as literals"
141	      b: "A wrapper object distinguishes refs from literals (e.g. { $ref: 'writ' } vs plain values)"
142	      c: "All string values are checked against a known variable set; unknown strings are passed as literals"
143	    selected: a
144	    analysis:
145	      audience: [patron, operator]
146	      confidence: high
147	      context: "The brief explicitly uses '$writ', '$role', '$buildCommand', '$testCommand' syntax. Option A is the simplest implementation that matches the brief. Option B is more explicit but adds JSON noise. Option C is equivalent to A for the known set. Since this is guild.json config (JSON), the $ convention is natural and readable."
148	      observable: true
149	      recommendation: a
150	      rationale: "Brief uses $-prefix syntax directly; simplest JSON-friendly representation"
151	      stakes: high
152	
153	  - id: D10
154	    scope: [S2]
155	    question: "What is the complete set of supported variable references?"
156	    options:
157	      a: "Four variables: $writ (WritDoc), $role (SpiderConfig.role ?? 'artificer'), $buildCommand (SpiderConfig.buildCommand), $testCommand (SpiderConfig.testCommand)"
158	      b: "Same four plus $codex (writ.codex) as a convenience"
159	    selected: a
160	    analysis:
161	      audience: [patron, operator]
162	      confidence: high
163	      context: "The brief lists exactly four variables. $codex can be accessed via $writ.codex in engine code — no need for a shortcut. The brief calls this 'a small set' and positions it as a stop-gap. Adding more variables without being asked adds surface area to maintain through the future needs-discovery migration."
164	      observable: true
165	      recommendation: a
166	      rationale: "Brief lists exactly four; stop-gap feature should have minimal surface area"
167	      stakes: low
168	
169	  - id: D11
170	    scope: [S2]
171	    question: "How are undefined variables handled?"
172	    options:
173	      a: "Omit the key entirely from the resolved givensSpec — if $buildCommand is undefined, the givensSpec has no 'buildCommand' key"
174	      b: "Set the key to null in givensSpec"
175	      c: "Set the key to undefined (which JSON.stringify would strip anyway)"
176	    selected: a
177	    analysis:
178	      audience: [author, operator]
179	      confidence: high
180	      context: "The brief says 'undefined variables are omitted from givens'. This matches the existing buildStaticEngines pattern: `...(config.buildCommand !== undefined ? { buildCommand: config.buildCommand } : {})`. Option A is consistent with both the brief and the existing code."
181	      observable: true
182	      recommendation: a
183	      rationale: "Brief is explicit: 'omitted from givens'; matches existing buildStaticEngines conditional-spread pattern"
184	      stakes: high
185	
186	  - id: D12
187	    scope: [S2]
188	    question: "Can template givens contain literal (non-variable) values?"
189	    options:
190	      a: "Yes — any value that isn't a $-prefixed string is passed through as a literal to givensSpec"
191	      b: "No — all givens values must be $-variable references"
192	    selected: a
193	    analysis:
194	      audience: [operator]
195	      confidence: high
196	      context: "The existing review engine receives `role: 'reviewer'` as a literal string in givensSpec (not from config). Templates need the same ability — e.g. a review engine that always uses a specific role regardless of the Spider's configured role. Allowing literals is essential for reproducing the current static pipeline's givens in template form."
197	      observable: true
198	      recommendation: a
199	      rationale: "Essential for reproducing the static pipeline's literal givens (e.g. role: 'reviewer') in template config"
200	      stakes: high
201	
202	  - id: D13
203	    scope: [S2]
204	    question: "What happens when a givens value is a $-prefixed string that is NOT a recognized variable (e.g. '$unknown')?"
205	    options:
206	      a: "Treat it as a literal string — pass '$unknown' through to givensSpec unchanged"
207	      b: "Treat it as an undefined variable — omit the key from givensSpec"
208	      c: "Fail validation at startup — unknown $-variables are config errors"
209	    selected: c
210	    analysis:
211	      audience: [operator]
212	      confidence: medium
213	      context: "If an operator writes '$buldCommand' (typo for '$buildCommand'), option A would silently pass the literal string '$buldCommand' to the engine, which would never be the intent. Option C catches this at startup. The set of valid variables is small and fixed, so validation is cheap and prevents silent misconfiguration."
214	      observable: true
215	      recommendation: c
216	      rationale: "Catches typos in variable references at startup rather than producing confusing runtime behavior"
217	      stakes: high
218	
219	  # ── S3: Startup validation ──────────────────────────────────────────
220	
221	  - id: D14
222	    scope: [S3]
223	    question: "When does template validation run — during start() or deferred?"
224	    options:
225	      a: "During start() — check designIds against fabricator.getEngineDesign() plus the Spider's own known supportKit engine IDs"
226	      b: "Deferred via ctx.on('plugin:initialized') — validate after Spider's own plugin:initialized fires (Fabricator registers Spider engines first in handler order)"
227	      c: "Lazy on first trySpawn() call — validate when a rig is first needed"
228	    selected: a
229	    analysis:
230	      audience: [patron, operator]
231	      confidence: high
232	      context: >
233	        The brief says 'invalid templates fail guild startup'. The inventory identified a timing issue: Spider's own engines (draft, implement, etc.) aren't in the Fabricator at start() time because they're registered via plugin:initialized after start() returns. However, the Spider imports its own engine objects and knows their IDs. At start() time: (1) kit-contributed engines are already in the Fabricator, (2) engines from apparatus started before Spider are in the Fabricator, (3) Spider's own engines are available as imports. So the Spider can validate designIds by checking fabricator.getEngineDesign(id) OR checking against its own supportKit engine IDs. This covers all engines that could legally be referenced — any engine from an apparatus starting after Spider wouldn't be available to the Spider anyway.
234	      observable: true
235	      recommendation: a
236	      rationale: "Validates at start() with no timing issues by combining Fabricator lookup with Spider's own known engine IDs"
237	      stakes: high
238	
239	  - id: D15
240	    scope: [S3]
241	    question: "How does the Spider know its own engine design IDs for start()-time validation?"
242	    options:
243	      a: "Build a Set from the imported engine objects: new Set([draftEngine.id, implementEngine.id, ...]) — the Spider already imports all five"
244	      b: "Hardcode a const set: new Set(['draft', 'implement', 'review', 'revise', 'seal'])"
245	      c: "Extract from the supportKit.engines keys in the return object"
246	    selected: a
247	    analysis:
248	      audience: [implementer]
249	      confidence: high
250	      context: "The Spider already imports all five engine objects at module scope for the supportKit. Using their .id property is reliable and self-maintaining — if an engine ID changes, this automatically tracks it. Option B is fragile. Option C requires restructuring the return object to extract supportKit before the return statement."
251	      observable: false
252	      recommendation: a
253	      rationale: "Self-maintaining: derives from the actual engine objects the Spider already imports"
254	      stakes: low
255	
256	  - id: D16
257	    scope: [S3]
258	    question: "What structural validations are performed on each template?"
259	    options:
260	      a: "Four checks: (1) non-empty engines array, (2) no duplicate engine IDs, (3) all upstream refs point to IDs within the same template, (4) dependency graph is acyclic"
261	      b: "Same as A plus (5) resolutionEngine (if specified) must be an ID within the template"
262	      c: "Same as B plus (6) at least one engine must have empty upstream (a root node)"
263	    selected: b
264	    analysis:
265	      audience: [operator]
266	      confidence: high
267	      context: "Checks 1-4 are the minimum for a valid DAG. Check 5 ensures the resolutionEngine reference is valid. Check 6 (root node) is implied by the acyclicity check — an acyclic non-empty graph always has at least one node with no incoming edges. So C is redundant."
268	      observable: true
269	      recommendation: b
270	      rationale: "Covers all structural invariants without redundant checks; includes resolutionEngine validation"
271	      stakes: low
272	
273	  - id: D17
274	    scope: [S3]
275	    question: "What cycle detection algorithm should be used?"
276	    options:
277	      a: "DFS-based topological sort with visited/visiting sets — same pattern used in arbor's guild-lifecycle.ts validateRequires()"
278	      b: "Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based, count in-degrees)"
279	    selected: a
280	    analysis:
281	      audience: [implementer]
282	      confidence: high
283	      context: "The codebase already uses DFS cycle detection in guild-lifecycle.ts (lines 76-98). Following the same pattern keeps the codebase consistent and is proven correct in this project."
284	      observable: false
285	      recommendation: a
286	      rationale: "Matches the existing cycle detection pattern in guild-lifecycle.ts"
287	      stakes: low
288	
289	  - id: D18
290	    scope: [S3]
291	    question: "What is the error message format for validation failures?"
292	    options:
293	      a: "Prefix with [spider] and include the template key and specific problem: '[spider] rigTemplates.mandate: designId \"foo\" not found in engine registry'"
294	      b: "Throw a generic Error with a descriptive message, no prefix"
295	    selected: a
296	    analysis:
297	      audience: [operator]
298	      confidence: high
299	      context: "Arbor's validateRequires uses '[arbor]' prefix. The Spider should follow the same pattern with '[spider]' prefix. Including the template key (writ type) localizes the error for operators with multiple templates."
300	      observable: true
301	      recommendation: a
302	      rationale: "Follows the [arbor] prefix convention; includes template key for localized error messages"
303	      stakes: low
304	
305	  - id: D19
306	    scope: [S3]
307	    question: "Does validation check all templates and report all errors, or fail on the first error?"
308	    options:
309	      a: "Fail on first error — throw immediately when the first invalid template is found"
310	      b: "Collect all errors across all templates and throw one combined error"
311	    selected: a
312	    analysis:
313	      audience: [operator]
314	      confidence: medium
315	      context: "The arbor validateRequires function fails on the first error. Collecting all errors is more user-friendly but adds complexity. Following the existing pattern keeps the implementation simple and consistent."
316	      observable: true
317	      recommendation: a
318	      rationale: "Matches the existing validateRequires fail-fast pattern"
319	      stakes: low
320	
321	  # ── S4: CDC resolution fallback ─────────────────────────────────────
322	
323	  - id: D20
324	    scope: [S4]
325	    question: "How does the CDC handler determine which engine's yields to use for the rig completion resolution?"
326	    options:
327	      a: "Check for a resolutionEngine field on the template config, then try id='seal', then fall back to the last engine in the engines array that has status='completed'"
328	      b: "Check for a resolutionEngine field stored on RigDoc at spawn time, then fall back to the last completed engine"
329	      c: "Always use the last completed engine in the engines array (no resolutionEngine concept)"
330	    selected: a
331	    analysis:
332	      audience: [patron, operator]
333	      confidence: medium
334	      context: >
335	        The brief offers two options: 'use the last completed engine's yields, or allow the template to declare which engine provides the resolution'. Option A implements both. It reads resolutionEngine from the template config (looked up via the writ's type at CDC time) rather than storing it on RigDoc (option B), avoiding RigDoc schema changes. The id='seal' check maintains backwards compat for the hardcoded pipeline. The last-completed-engine fallback handles templates that don't declare a resolution engine. Since CDC fires at rig completion time, we need the config to be available — it is, because spiderConfig is captured at start() time.
336	      observable: true
337	      recommendation: a
338	      rationale: "Implements both brief options without RigDoc changes; seal check preserves backwards compat"
339	      stakes: high
340	
341	  - id: D21
342	    scope: [S4]
343	    question: "How is 'last completed engine' determined for the fallback?"
344	    options:
345	      a: "Last engine in the engines array with status='completed' — array order from the template defines the sequence"
346	      b: "Engine with the latest completedAt timestamp"
347	    selected: a
348	    analysis:
349	      audience: [implementer]
350	      confidence: high
351	      context: "The engines array is ordered by the template definition. In a linear pipeline, the last-in-array completed engine is the terminal one. For DAGs with parallel branches, array order is a reasonable convention since completedAt timing is non-deterministic. Array order is simpler and deterministic."
352	      observable: true
353	      recommendation: a
354	      rationale: "Deterministic and simple; array order from template definition is the natural sequence"
355	      stakes: low
356	
357	  - id: D22
358	    scope: [S4]
359	    question: "How does the CDC handler look up the template to find resolutionEngine?"
360	    options:
361	      a: "Look up writ.type from the writ (already available via rig.writId → writsBook), then look up the template from spiderConfig.rigTemplates[writ.type] with same fallback chain as spawn"
362	      b: "Store the resolutionEngine value on RigDoc at spawn time so the CDC handler doesn't need to look up the template"
363	    selected: b
364	    analysis:
365	      audience: [implementer]
366	      confidence: medium
367	      context: >
368	        Option A requires the CDC handler to read the writ and traverse the template fallback chain — a lot of logic in a hot CDC path. It also has a subtle bug: if the config changes between spawn and rig completion, the wrong template could be looked up. Option B adds one optional field to RigDoc but makes the CDC handler simple and correct — it reads rig.resolutionEngine, done. This is a small, safe RigDoc addition (optional field, no index, no migration needed for existing rigs which won't have it).
369	      observable: false
370	      recommendation: b
371	      rationale: "Avoids config-drift bugs and keeps the CDC handler simple; one optional field on RigDoc is low-cost"
372	      stakes: high
373	
374	  - id: D23
375	    scope: [S4]
376	    question: "What field name on RigDoc stores the resolution engine ID?"
377	    options:
378	      a: "resolutionEngineId — explicit about what it references"
379	      b: "resolutionEngine — matches the template field name"
380	    selected: a
381	    analysis:
382	      audience: [implementer]
383	      confidence: high
384	      context: "RigDoc already has fields like writId (not writ). The Id suffix is consistent with the document's naming convention and clearly distinguishes the reference from an actual engine object."
385	      observable: false
386	      recommendation: a
387	      rationale: "Consistent with RigDoc's writId naming pattern"
388	      stakes: low
389	
390	  # ── S5: Backwards compatibility ─────────────────────────────────────
391	
392	  - id: D24
393	    scope: [S5]
394	    question: "Is buildStaticEngines() preserved as a separate function, or replaced by template-based building?"
395	    options:
396	      a: "Preserve buildStaticEngines() as-is; the new dispatch function calls it when no template matches"
397	      b: "Express the hardcoded 5-engine pipeline as an internal DEFAULT_TEMPLATE constant and route all spawns through the template builder"
398	    selected: a
399	    analysis:
400	      audience: [implementer]
401	      confidence: medium
402	      context: "Option B is more elegant (single code path) but risks behavioral drift if the template builder has subtle differences from the original function. Option A guarantees zero behavior change for existing guilds with no template config — the hardcoded function is unchanged. The brief emphasizes 'full backwards compatibility'."
403	      observable: false
404	      recommendation: a
405	      rationale: "Zero-risk backwards compat: original function untouched for the no-template path"
406	      stakes: high
407	
408	  - id: D25
409	    scope: [S5, S1]
410	    question: "What is the name for the new dispatch function that selects template vs static?"
411	    options:
412	      a: "buildRigEngines(writ, config) — replaces the call site in trySpawn()"
413	      b: "resolveEngines(writ, config) — emphasizes the resolution/lookup aspect"
414	    selected: a
415	    analysis:
416	      audience: [implementer]
417	      confidence: high
418	      context: "The existing function is buildStaticEngines. buildRigEngines follows the same naming pattern and clearly indicates it builds the engine list for a rig."
419	      observable: false
420	      recommendation: a
421	      rationale: "Follows the existing buildStaticEngines naming pattern"
422	      stakes: low
423	
424	  - id: D26
425	    scope: [S5, S1]
426	    question: "What does buildRigEngines return when using a template — should it also set resolutionEngineId?"
427	    options:
428	      a: "Return { engines: EngineInstance[], resolutionEngineId?: string } — a tuple so trySpawn can set both on RigDoc"
429	      b: "Return just EngineInstance[] and have trySpawn separately look up resolutionEngine from the template"
430	    selected: a
431	    analysis:
432	      audience: [implementer]
433	      confidence: high
434	      context: "The resolution engine ID is determined during template resolution. Returning it alongside the engines keeps the logic centralized in one function rather than having trySpawn re-derive the template."
435	      observable: false
436	      recommendation: a
437	      rationale: "Keeps template resolution logic centralized; trySpawn just assigns the result"
438	      stakes: low
439	
440	  - id: D27
441	    scope: [S1, S2]
442	    question: "How is the new buildTemplateEngines function factored — one function or split into template lookup + variable resolution + engine construction?"
443	    options:
444	      a: "One function: resolveTemplate(writ, config) → { engines, resolutionEngineId? } that handles lookup, variable resolution, and EngineInstance construction"
445	      b: "Three functions: lookupTemplate(writType, config) → RigTemplate | null, resolveGivens(givens, variables) → Record<string,unknown>, buildFromTemplate(template, variables) → EngineInstance[]"
446	    selected: b
447	    analysis:
448	      audience: [implementer]
449	      confidence: high
450	      context: "Split functions are independently testable. resolveGivens in particular has distinct logic (variable detection, undefined omission) that benefits from isolated unit tests. lookupTemplate encapsulates the fallback chain. buildFromTemplate is the structural mapper."
451	      observable: false
452	      recommendation: b
453	      rationale: "Each function is independently testable with distinct concerns"
454	      stakes: low
455	
456	  - id: D28
457	    scope: [S4, S5]
458	    question: "For the hardcoded-pipeline path (no templates configured), what resolutionEngineId is set on RigDoc?"
459	    options:
460	      a: "Set resolutionEngineId to 'seal' — explicit even for the hardcoded path"
461	      b: "Leave resolutionEngineId undefined — the CDC handler falls back to id='seal' check then last-completed-engine"
462	    selected: a
463	    analysis:
464	      audience: [implementer]
465	      confidence: high
466	      context: "Setting it explicitly for the hardcoded path means the CDC handler has one consistent code path: check rig.resolutionEngineId. The special-case seal check in D20 becomes the fallback for old rigs that were created before this feature (which won't have the field). New rigs — whether from templates or the hardcoded pipeline — always have the field set."
467	      observable: false
468	      recommendation: a
469	      rationale: "Consistent behavior: all new rigs set the field; seal fallback only needed for pre-existing rigs"
470	      stakes: low
471	
472	  - id: D29
473	    scope: [S1]
474	    question: "Does the start() method remain synchronous (void) or become async (Promise<void>)?"
475	    options:
476	      a: "Remains synchronous — template validation is pure computation over in-memory data (config + fabricator registry)"
477	      b: "Becomes async — to allow future validation that requires I/O"
478	    selected: a
479	    analysis:
480	      audience: [implementer]
481	      confidence: high
482	      context: "The Apparatus.start type allows both void and Promise<void>. Template validation only reads spiderConfig (already parsed) and calls fabricator.getEngineDesign (synchronous Map lookup). No I/O needed. Keeping it sync is simpler and avoids unnecessary async overhead."
483	      observable: false
484	      recommendation: a
485	      rationale: "All validation inputs are synchronous in-memory lookups; no reason to introduce async"
486	      stakes: low
